street.thesis: Netflix has built the only scaled, profitable streaming platform; advertising and price hike velocity should drive revenue re-acceleration to 15%+ while margins expand toward 35%+. The 40% drawdown from the June 2025 high is a valuation opportunity, not a fundamental deterioration. FY2026 guidance maintained; FCF compounding from $9.5B to $12.5B+.

history_context.note: Netflix FCF CAGR 2022-2025: from $1.6B (FY2022) to $9.5B (FY2025) = approximately 82%/year absolute growth — driven by margin expansion from 17.8% to 29.5%. This is not a steady-state CAGR; it reflects operating leverage kicking in. On revenue: 16%/year CAGR FY2022-2025. The FCF CAGR is misleading as a forward guide — the right anchor is the FY2026 guided $12.5B growing toward ~$18B by FY2028.

duality_decomposition.note: single-stage justified forward P/E = (1 - g/ROIC)/(r - g); shows the ROIC/payout lever

peers.name: Spotify
peers.note: Audio streaming analog; 20% revenue growth; margin expansion story similar to Netflix 2020-2023. Higher multiple reflects faster growth rate from smaller base.

peers.name: Walt Disney (streaming segments only)
peers.note: Not a clean comp (legacy parks/linear); at 4% revenue growth it is a mature conglomerate. Netflix deserves a premium.

peers.name: Amazon (blended, for Prime Video context)
peers.note: Amazon is growing at 10% total with high-margin AWS. Netflix is not a clean Amazon comp but useful for advertising platform context.

reconstruction.what_price_implies: At 27.9x NTM FCF (FY2026 guided), the current price implies a ~8.5% FCF CAGR for 10y (then 4% terminal) at r=9.3%.
reconstruction.what_street_implies: The street mean target implies ~13.0% for 10y.
reconstruction.history_anchor: Netflix FCF CAGR 2022-2025: from $1.6B (FY2022) to $9.5B (FY2025) = approximately 82%/year absolute growth — driven by margin expansion from 17.8% to 29.5%. This is not a steady-state CAGR; it reflects operating leverage kicking in. On revenue: 16%/year CAGR FY2022-2025. The FCF CAGR is misleading as a forward guide — the right anchor is the FY2026 guided $12.5B growing toward ~$18B by FY2028.
reconstruction.contested_lever: Whether advertising ARPU per ad-tier subscriber scales from ~$15-20/year today toward $40-55/year by 2028-2030 — i.e., can Netflix's in-house ad tech achieve the fill rates and CPMs that justify a 15-18x EBIT multiple on the advertising segment's $6.5B in 2028 earnings contribution, or does Amazon's structural data advantage cap Netflix's ad yield and compress the platform re-rate?
